case_name: MTBE-contaminated reservoir water treatment under seasonal variability, cost, and energy constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for MTBE-impacted reservoir water intended for drinking water supply while balancing seasonal raw water variability, cost limits, and energy sensitivity.

input_data.scenario_background: A medium-sized utility draws from a reservoir affected by intermittent gasoline-related runoff from a marina and nearby transportation corridor, leading to recurring MTBE detections and periodic taste-and-odor complaints. Raw water quality varies strongly by season: spring turnover and storm events increase turbidity and natural organic matter, while summer conditions raise algae-related organics and increase volatilization concerns. The utility can fund a moderate upgrade but is sensitive to long-term power use and does not want a treatment train that becomes difficult to operate during variable reservoir conditions. Several technologies can address MTBE, but the preferred choice must balance contaminant control, robustness to changing water quality, cost, and energy demand.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should reliably achieve potable compliance and control MTBE to below taste-and-odor concern levels with stable performance across seasonal reservoir conditions.
secondary_goals: handle variable turbidity and organic matter without major performance loss

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer to avoid large-volume liquid concentrate streams; manageable spent media or off-gas controls are acceptable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: treatment must tolerate seasonal shifts in turbidity, NOM, and occasional contaminant spikes without frequent shutdowns or excessive retuning

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove seasonal particulate matter and reduce NOM load to stabilize downstream MTBE treatment
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish solids removal and protect stripping and adsorption units
core_unit_functions.air_stripping_with_off_gas_control: remove the bulk of volatile MTBE and co-occurring gasoline-related VOCs
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_polishing: provide polishing for residual VOCs and support taste-and-odor control
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety for potable supply

applicability_rationale: MTBE is a volatile oxygenate, so air stripping is a technically suitable primary removal step, especially when paired with off-gas management
upstream clarification and filtration improve reliability under seasonal reservoir turbidity and NOM swings
GAC polishing adds resilience for residual organics, including geosmin, MIB, and remaining BTEX fractions
this hybrid route balances effectiveness with lower energy demand than advanced oxidation or membrane systems
the process is more robust to fluctuating raw water than a polishing-only adsorption approach
potential_risks: air emissions control is required because contaminants are transferred to off-gas rather than destroyed
MTBE is more water-soluble than many gasoline hydrocarbons, so stripping performance must be conservatively designed
GAC replacement frequency may increase during high NOM or taste-and-odor seasons

core_unit_functions.enhanced_coagulation: reduce NOM and seasonal organics that compete for adsorption capacity
core_unit_functions.sedimentation_and_filtration: remove solids and protect GAC beds
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_dual_bed: adsorb MTBE residuals and co-contaminants while also helping with taste-and-odor compounds

applicability_rationale: GAC is a familiar drinking water technology and can fit utilities seeking lower energy demand than stripping towers with significant air handling or advanced oxidation
dual-bed staging improves breakthrough management under changing source water conditions
this route gives co-benefits for algae-related organics and customer aesthetic concerns
it is operationally simpler than ozone-biological systems or high-pressure membranes
potential_risks: MTBE is relatively weakly adsorbed compared with many hydrophobic organics, so carbon usage can be high
high seasonal DOC and taste-and-odor episodes can shorten bed life and raise operating cost
full-scale performance may be less economical than stripping when MTBE is the dominant target contaminant

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce seasonal particles and part of the organic load
core_unit_functions.filtration: provide solids polishing before oxidation and biological adsorption
core_unit_functions.ozonation: transform selected organics and improve biodegradability
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: biologically and adsorptively polish residual organics, taste-and-odor compounds, and oxidation byproducts

applicability_rationale: ozone-BAC is a plausible multi-benefit route where the utility also wants seasonal taste-and-odor control and broader organic polishing
the train can perform well for variable reservoir water if properly designed and monitored
it can be attractive when treatment goals extend beyond MTBE alone
potential_risks: oxidation of MTBE may be incomplete without sufficient contact and control, so relying on ozone alone is risky
bromate formation risk must be managed if bromide is present, even at low to moderate levels
biological filter management increases operator attention and process monitoring needs
power demand is higher than simpler adsorption-focused alternatives

core_unit_functions.pretreatment_filtration: reduce turbidity and improve UV transmittance stability
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process_uv_hydrogen_p: oxidatively degrade MTBE and some co-occurring organics
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: polish oxidation byproducts and residual organics

applicability_rationale: AOP is technically capable for recalcitrant organic contaminants and can directly destroy rather than transfer MTBE
it may be justified where destruction is strongly preferred over off-gas handling
the polishing GAC step can address byproducts and aesthetic residuals
potential_risks: high energy demand conflicts with the stated utility constraint
variable reservoir water quality, especially DOC and UV absorbance, can increase oxidant dose and reduce process economy
process control is more complex than stripping or conventional adsorption
byproduct management and quenching requirements add operational burden

core_unit_functions.conventional_pretreatment: protect membrane system from fouling by suspended solids and organics
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: separate a broad range of dissolved contaminants through high-pressure membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry for finished water distribution compatibility

applicability_rationale: RO is included as a technically strong but constraint-misaligned option because it can provide broad contaminant control if many dissolved pollutants are of concern
it may appeal where future source degradation could require a barrier for multiple constituents
potential_risks: high-pressure operation and concentrate management are difficult to justify for a primarily volatile organic contaminant problem
seasonal reservoir fouling potential increases pretreatment and cleaning burden
the process is overdesigned relative to the main MTBE treatment need and conflicts with cost and energy sensitivity

preferred_rank_pattern: air_stripping_based_routes should rank near the top for MTBE-dominant VOC treatment
GAC-based routes should appear as credible alternatives but not necessarily rank first where MTBE is the main driver and NOM is moderate to elevated
advanced oxidation should be recognized as technically capable but downgraded under strong energy sensitivity
reverse_osmosis should not rank first because it is misaligned with a volatile contaminant problem and concentrate constraints

must_reflect: seasonal reservoir variability requires robust pretreatment ahead of core MTBE removal
energy sensitivity should penalize AOP and membrane-intensive options
cost limits should favor simpler mass-transfer or adsorption approaches over highly engineered destruction or desalting systems

must_contain: why MTBE volatility supports air stripping as a primary treatment candidate
why elevated seasonal NOM and turbidity affect GAC consumption and pretreatment needs
why technically capable oxidation or membrane options are ranked lower due to cost and energy misfit
why co-occurring taste-and-odor compounds influence the value of GAC or BAC polishing

should_mention: adsorbent breakthrough and higher media usage under elevated DOC conditions
AOP sensitivity to water matrix and byproduct control
membrane concentrate disposal and fouling burden
